2606.21306.title: Towards Dys-XAI: Influence-Based Explanations for Dysarthria Severity Assessment
2606.21306.paper_summary: Dysarthria severity assessment is essential for therapy planning and longitudinal monitoring, yet manual perceptual rating is time-consuming and variable across clinicians. Although deep learning models achieve strong performance, their black-box nature limits clinical adoption. Existing speech explainability methods typically provide acoustic feature importance scores that are difficult for end-users to interpret. We propose an influence-based, instance-level explainability framework that explains each decision through supportive and competing training samples. Using gradient-based influence approximations, we compute per-utterance influence scores to identify supportive and competing training samples for each prediction. Controlled deletion experiments from 5 to 20 percent validate the explanations, showing that removing highly influential samples systematically shifts predictions. This approach provides auditable explanations by linking decisions to perceptible reference cases.
2606.21306.paper_summary_zh: 語音障礙的嚴重程度評估對於治療計劃和長期監測至關重要，然而手動感知評分既耗時又因臨床醫生而異。雖然深度學習模型表現出色，但其黑箱特性限制了臨床應用。現有的語音可解釋性方法通常提供難以讓最終用戶解釋的聲學特徵重要性分數。我們提出了一種基於影響的實例級可解釋性框架，通過支持性和競爭性訓練樣本來解釋每個決策。使用基於梯度的影響近似，我們計算每次發話的影響分數，以識別每個預測的支持性和競爭性訓練樣本。從5%到20%的控制刪除實驗驗證了解釋，顯示移除高度影響樣本會系統性地改變預測。這種方法通過將決策與可感知的參考案例聯繫起來，提供可審計的解釋。
2606.21306.author: Xiaoliang Wu et.al.
2606.21306.authors: Xiaoliang Wu, Qiyang Sun, Yupei Li, Erfan Loweimi, Jennifer Williams, Zhengjun Yue
